questions.question: A service that makes 1,000 outbound connections per second fails at peak load with EADDRNOTAVAIL. The default ip_local_port_range is 32768-60999. What is the cause?
options: B. Each closed connection enters TIME_WAIT for 60s; 1000/s * 60s = 60,000 ports needed; only 28,232 are available. The kernel cannot find an unused port.
C. The TCP MSS is wrong
D. The application is using too much CPU
questions.explanation: Each closed connection enters TIME_WAIT for 2*MSL (60s on Linux). 1000 connections/s * 60s = 60,000 ports in TIME_WAIT at any moment. The default ip_local_port_range 32768-60999 only provides 28,232 ports. The fix is to expand the range, enable tcp_tw_reuse, and reduce tcp_fin_timeout.

questions.question: What does tcp_tw_reuse=1 do, and what is the safety constraint?
options: B. It allows the kernel to recycle a TIME_WAIT socket for a new outgoing connection to the SAME destination IP:port; the safety constraint is that the peer has already moved on, so straggling packets are ignored
C. It disables TCP congestion control
questions.explanation: tcp_tw_reuse=1 allows the kernel to assign a TIME_WAIT socket to a new outgoing connection if the destination is the same IP:port. The constraint (same destination) is safe because the peer has already moved on, so any straggling packets from the old connection are ignored.

questions.question: Why was tcp_tw_recycle=1 removed in Linux 4.12?
options: B. It caused widespread breakage for NATed clients; the TIME_WAIT shortcut applied to incoming connections, but NATs make many internal hosts look like the same IP, breaking their flows
C. It was a security vulnerability
questions.explanation: tcp_tw_recycle=1 would skip TIME_WAIT for incoming connections from the same IP within a recent window. NATs make many internal hosts look like the same external IP, so the shortcut would break the flows of many clients sharing a NAT. The option was removed in Linux 4.12 (commit 5d2ed0527c).
